cases.title: Trump v. Cook, No. 25A312 (U.S. June 29, 2026)
cases.court: Supreme Court of the United States

sections.heading: 1. The Issue
sections.content_markdown:
Whether a District Court's preliminary injunction restoring Lisa Cook — a Federal Reserve Governor whom the President purported to fire for alleged pre-office mortgage fraud — to her position pending merits litigation should be stayed. Three sub-questions carry the analysis: **(1)** whether the Federal Reserve Act's "for cause" removal provision (12 U.S.C. § 242) is judicially reviewable at all; **(2)** whether the statute implicitly requires **notice + opportunity to respond** before removal, given its fixed-14-year-term structure; and **(3)** whether federal courts have **equitable authority** to enter interim reinstatement relief against the President's removal of a principal executive officer.

Underlying the whole case: whether the Federal Reserve — decided the same day in the companion case *Trump v. Slaughter* — falls within a **historical-tradition carveout** to that day's newly-announced general rule that principal executive officers must be removable at will.

sections.heading: 2. Rule Mapping
sections.content_markdown:
**Statutory removal provision (Federal Reserve Act, 12 U.S.C. § 242):** "Each member shall hold office for a term of fourteen years from the expiration of the term of his predecessor, unless sooner removed for cause by the President." The word "cause" is undefined; the statute is silent as to procedure.

**Common-law "cause" as term of art (backdrop rule):** When Congress legislates "against the backdrop of the common law" — *Comcast Corp. v. National Assn. of African American-Owned Media*, 589 U.S. 327, 335 (2020) — courts read common-law terms per their settled common-law meaning. *Jam v. International Finance Corp.*, 586 U.S. 199, 211 (2019). "Cause" at common law required more than "any reason" — it required a "substantial, reasonable and just" ground amounting to a "disqualification" akin to inefficiency or incompetency. *In re Nichols*, 6 Abb. N. Cas. 474, 480 (NY Sup. Ct. 1879); *Board of Street Comm'rs of Hagerstown v. Williams*, 96 Md. 232, 236–239, 53 A. 923, 924–925 (1903). Contours depend on the "nature of the service to be performed" and "duties of the office."

**Fixed-term + for-cause = implicit notice-and-hearing (Reagan/Shurtleff rule):** "[T]he rule . . . is that notice and hearing are essential before an officer's removal where the term of office is for a fixed period." *Reagan v. United States*, 182 U.S. 419, 425 (1901); *Shurtleff v. United States*, 189 U.S. 311, 314 (1903). Congress is presumed to legislate against this baseline. *Williams v. Taylor*, 529 U.S. 420, 434 (2000). Minimal process required: **notice of charges + some opportunity to respond** (written materials sufficient; no full judicial trial). *Londoner v. City and County of Denver*, 210 U.S. 373, 386 (1908).

**Central-bank historical tradition (Federal Reserve carveout):** The Federal Reserve follows in a "distinct historical tradition" of independent central banking traced from the Bank of North America (1781) → First Bank of the United States (1791) → Second Bank of the United States (1816) → Federal Reserve System (1913, restructured 1933 and 1935). All three predecessors featured for-cause-equivalent protection from Presidential control, animated by Founders' fear (Hamilton in *Report on a National Bank* 331) of "suspicion" of political manipulation of monetary policy. *Seila Law LLC v. Consumer Financial Protection Bureau*, 591 U.S. 197, 222, n. 8 (2020) (naming Federal Reserve as example of an entity that "may not be encompassed by *Myers*'s general rule").

**Equity — pending-litigation protection of *de facto* officer:** Courts of equity historically could not adjudicate final title to office (a quo warranto or mandamus function of courts of law), but *could* "recogniz[e] and protec[t] the possession of officers de facto, . . . pending a litigation" at law "to determine their title." 2 J. High, *Law of Injunctions* § 1315, at 866 (2d ed. 1880). Distinct from *In re Sawyer*, 124 U.S. 200 (1888), and *White v. Berry*, 171 U.S. 366 (1898), which foreclosed final equitable relief where mandamus/quo warranto were adequate legal remedies.

**Stay standard:** Applicant must show (i) likelihood of success on merits, (ii) likelihood of certiorari grant, (iii) irreparable harm, (iv) balance of equities. *Hollingsworth v. Perry*, 558 U.S. 183, 190 (2010) (per curiam); *Nken v. Holder*, 556 U.S. 418, 434 (2009).

sections.heading: 3. Synthesis: The "Why" and "So What"
sections.content_markdown:
**The Why.** The majority (Roberts, C.J., for a 5-Justice Court) declines to grant the stay on the **narrowest possible ground**: the President did not afford Cook the pre-termination procedural protections that the Federal Reserve Act — read against the *Reagan/Shurtleff* backdrop — implicitly requires. That resolution is deliberately narrow: the Court explicitly avoids the broader constitutional question of whether *any* limitation on the President's removal power over Federal Reserve Governors is permissible, because *the Government did not challenge the statute's constitutionality* in this application. This is a rare instance of the Court honoring party presentation limits at the emergency-docket stage — Alito's dissent (joined by Gorsuch) and Barrett's dissent both criticize the majority for going too far even so; Thomas's dissent criticizes it for not going far enough (would hold the statute itself unconstitutional under Article II).

The three-part structure of the majority's holding is worth tracing because each part is a distinct doctrinal move:

- **Reviewability** (Part II-A). The Government argued the President's "cause" determination is *wholly* unreviewable — a textual commitment to Presidential discretion. The Court rejects this. Congress *could* have written the statute to make cause unreviewable ("Congress could of course afford the President the power to remove Federal Reserve Governors at will. Or Congress could exempt the President's removal of Governors for cause from judicial review. But Congress has done neither."). The Government's own concession that courts may review removals where the President "identifies no cause at all" defeats the position: **any judicial line-drawing requires statutory interpretation of what "cause" means**, which is a merits inquiry, not an unreviewability rule.

- **"Cause" as term of art requiring a substantial threshold** (Part II-B). Both parties get rejected. Government argues "cause" = any concern about conduct/ability/fitness/competence (too low). Cook argues "cause" = only the specific statutory grounds Congress used in *other* removal statutes as of 1935: inefficiency, neglect of duty, malfeasance, ineligibility (too high). The Court chooses **structural context over both extremes**: cause "must reflect the Federal Reserve's unique historical status and role," which counsels a *substantial* threshold. Whether cause exists depends on the **seriousness of the alleged misconduct** and the **extent of any nexus to the Governor's professional duties**. Key structural insight: "any perceived or alleged misstep (past or present) could provide a ready pretext for a Governor's removal — a fact that he would surely know, and that would surely weigh on him as he decided what to say and how to vote. Nothing could be more corrosive of the independence that Congress sought to preserve."

- **Notice + hearing implicit in fixed-term + for-cause** (Part III-A). This is the *Reagan/Shurtleff* rebuttable presumption applied straight: when Congress enacts a fixed-term + for-cause structure "against the backdrop of the common law," it incorporates the common-law rule requiring pre-termination notice and opportunity to respond. What's remarkable is Thomas's dissent characterizing this as a "single line of dicta" from two 1901/1903 cases; the majority responds that vertical stare decisis makes a Supreme Court statement about "the rule" load-bearing whether or not the officers in *Reagan* and *Shurtleff* ultimately won.

**The federal-reserve-tradition move** (Part III-A(2), Kavanaugh concurrence) is where *Cook* has to be read with *Slaughter*. Same-day, the *Slaughter* majority (also Roberts, C.J.) overrules *Humphrey's Executor* and holds that principal executive officers must generally be removable at will. But the *Slaughter* majority preserves a specific carveout for the Federal Reserve "to the extent that it follows in the distinct historical tradition of the First and Second Banks of the United States." *Cook* activates that carveout: the Federal Reserve's "founding-era pedigree" — Hamilton's *Report on a National Bank* + First Bank's private-direction structure + Second Bank's independence from Presidential control — constitutes the "essential content" that history provides "to undefined provisions in the frame of our government" (quoting Frankfurter's *Youngstown* concurrence). Kavanaugh's concurrence is explicit: after *Slaughter*, "there is a clear choice: Either the Federal Reserve may remain independent (with the Governors removable for cause, not at will), or it may not." He would not "risk destabilizing the U.S. economy" to leave that open.

**The So What.** Three propositions land as usable precedent:

1. **The Federal Reserve is constitutionally distinct.** After *Cook* + *Slaughter*, the Federal Reserve's for-cause protection is not just statutorily valid — it is constitutionally *sanctioned by history*. This is a **historical-tradition safe harbor** carved out of the *Slaughter* rule that Article II requires at-will removal of principal executive officers. Other agencies claiming similar protection will need to show the same kind of founding-era pedigree; nothing else in the majority opinion suggests the carveout extends beyond central banking.

2. **Fixed-term + for-cause statutes carry implicit procedural requirements.** *Reagan/Shurtleff* is reaffirmed and applied: Congress cannot enact a fixed-term-with-cause structure and simultaneously permit summary removal. Any officer whose statute has that structure (and there are still many — see Thomas's dissent Note 2 cataloging hundreds) has a pre-termination notice-and-hearing right *even if the statute doesn't say so explicitly*. This is a durable statutory-interpretation holding independent of the Federal Reserve carveout.

3. **Equity can protect de facto officers pending litigation.** *In re Sawyer* and *White v. Berry* are cabined to their facts (adequate legal remedies foreclosed equity there); interim reinstatement injunctions are permissible where the officer has a likely meritorious claim. Practical effect: officers wrongfully fired can seek and receive preliminary injunctions preserving their office during litigation, at least where Article III standing and statutory rights exist.

Downstream: expect litigation over *which* other agencies (if any) can claim the Federal Reserve's historical-tradition carveout. Thomas's dissent argues sharply that even the Federal Reserve doesn't qualify because it "is not a 'bank' but a novel federal agency" — that critique may resurface if a future case tests, say, the FDIC or the OCC.

sections.heading: 4. Logical Flaws & Observations
sections.content_markdown:
- **Party presentation at emergency-docket altitude.** The most striking structural feature of *Cook* is what the majority *doesn't* decide. The Government did not challenge the constitutionality of § 242's removal restriction. The majority accepts that concession and resolves the case on statutory grounds. Barrett's dissent argues the majority still goes too broadly (naming the historical-tradition carveout in Part III-A(2) despite the constitutional question being waived); Alito's dissent goes further, arguing the majority should not have opined on any issue beyond the two the D.C. Circuit resolved (due process + "cause" scope). This tension — between judicial restraint and interim-docket transparency — is the same tension Barrett named in *Mirabelli v. Bonta*, 607 U.S. 492 (2026): "lock-in risk of saying too much against the transparency cost of saying too little." *Cook* comes down closer to the "saying enough to be useful" side, and the meta-honesty note is that reasonable minds can disagree.

- **The "cause" definition the Court leaves open.** The majority "need not fully demarcate the contours of 'cause' today." That's *In re Nichols*-style deferential language, but it means the substantive standard remains to be fleshed out on remand. Lower courts now have to apply a "substantial threshold" reflecting the Federal Reserve's role — likely a two-step analysis: (a) seriousness of misconduct; (b) nexus to professional duties. Whether *Cook*'s alleged pre-office mortgage fraud clears that bar is the merits question the majority carefully does *not* resolve.

- **Thomas's Article II theory as inverse counterpoint.** Thomas's dissent is the pure Article II unitary-executive position: removal power is inherent in the vesting clause; Congress cannot limit it; the statute is unconstitutional. The dissent's historical work is thorough but faces the problem the majority names: the historical tradition of central bank independence *predates* the Constitution (Bank of North America 1781) and was reaffirmed by the same Founders who ratified Article II. This is one of the few areas where founding-era practice cuts *against* a strong unitary-executive reading, and Thomas's response — that the Federal Reserve isn't really analogous to the First/Second Banks — is precisely the fact-intensive line the majority declines to draw at this stage.

- **The equitable-relief holding is quietly load-bearing.** *In re Sawyer* and *White v. Berry* had been read broadly to foreclose equitable relief in officer-removal cases. *Cook* clarifies that these cases addressed *final* equitable relief where legal remedies (mandamus, quo warranto, backpay) were adequate — they do not foreclose *interim* protection of officers pending litigation. This preserves preliminary-injunction practice for wrongfully-removed officers, which had been in real doubt after *Wilcox* and *Boyle* stay orders.

- **The stale-hash procedural detail worth naming.** The dispute over notice-and-hearing carries a substantive undercurrent: the majority (Part III-B) rejects the Government's argument that Trump's social-media post ("Cook must resign, now!!!") constituted adequate notice. What "will not do" is a post that "did not suggest that a response from Cook would be appropriate, nor did it even provide a clear account of the charge made against her." The rule that emerges: notice must include (i) some explanation of evidence at issue, (ii) some avenue for response, (iii) a deadline. *Mullane v. Central Hanover Bank & Trust Co.*, 339 U.S. 306, 314–315 (1950) is invoked by analogy. This is the shape of due process bleeding into statutory-notice doctrine.

sections.heading: 5. Follow-ups for Mr. C
sections.content_markdown:
- **Doctrinal-scaffold angle (for JR's returning-paralegal register):** The core structural move in *Cook* is a **presumption + backdrop-rebuttal** pattern. Congress enacts fixed-term + for-cause → *presumed* to legislate against the common-law backdrop (*Comcast*, *Jam*) → common-law backdrop is *Reagan/Shurtleff*'s notice-and-hearing rule → therefore statute implicitly requires notice-and-hearing. This is the same shape as the FIAFEA presumption-of-review rebuttal analysis in [[24-1990|Jacobson v. United States]] and the "true nature" doctrine in [[24-2242|Erdemir v. ITC]] — Congress presumed to write against a settled backdrop, backdrop displaced only by clear-and-convincing showing to the contrary. **Worth naming as a discrete category:** "backdrop rebuttable presumptions" as a distinct interpretive doctrine.

- **Weight-of-words angle:** "For cause" carries centuries of common-law weight — it is not a phrase Congress uses lightly. The majority's move is to make that weight legible: "Congress enacted this statute 'against the backdrop of the common law,' such that we must look to the common law to decipher what 'cause' (a term of art) requires." Two words with statutory weight; two centuries of case law loaded into them. This is the same discipline as reading "true nature" (Erdemir), "arbitrary and capricious" (APA), or "reasonable" (contract law) — none of which mean what plain-English reading would suggest. **The lesson worth naming:** statutory terms with common-law pedigrees are never plain-English; they carry their history.

- **Structural analogy (rigid statutes, flexible analysis):** *Cook* is a beautiful example of the rigid-in-statutes-but-flexible-in-analysis pattern you named in your legal-background reflection. The statutory language is fixed ("for cause"), but its application to the Federal Reserve's unique historical role is deeply flexible — the "substantial threshold" the Court identifies depends on the "nature of the service to be performed" and "duties of the office." Same phrase, different bite in different institutional contexts. Compare to the Federal Circuit's willingness to distinguish *Fisher* in [[24-1990|Jacobson]] based on whether jurisdiction blends with merits — same doctrinal source, differently binding depending on structural shape.

- **The historical-tradition safe harbor as a new (or newly-explicit) doctrine.** *Slaughter* announces a general rule (Article II requires at-will removal of principal executive officers) but preserves specific historical-tradition carveouts (Federal Reserve; non-Article III courts reserved). *Cook* activates the Federal Reserve carveout. This is a doctrinal shape worth naming because it will recur: "general rule + historical-tradition carveout" is how the Court is going to handle other structural-Constitution questions where founding-era practice cuts against modern theory. Watch for it in future terms.

- **Cross-stitch with prior corpus.** *Cook* + *Slaughter* together sit at the top of the removal-law doctrinal tree; every future case in this space (agency independence, civil service, MSPB, adjudicative bodies) will trace back here. Pair with *Free Enterprise Fund*, *Seila Law*, *Collins v. Yellen*, *Wilcox*, and *Boyle* to see the compound arc: from *Humphrey's Executor* (1935) → *Wiener* (1958) → *Free Enterprise Fund* (2010) → *Seila Law* (2020) → *Slaughter* + *Cook* (2026). **Recommend an SR Sharpens pass on this arc as a foundational doctrinal-map exercise** — it teaches Article II removal law through evolution, which is exactly how doctrine is best learned from ground zero.

- **Field Clerk paired 2026-07-11:** [[25A312|Field Clerk Draft — 25A312 (Trump v. Cook)]] at `Terminal/Central Hub/research/data/legal/field-clerks/25A312.md`. Isolates four carry-forward doctrines: (i) backdrop rebuttable presumption (**promoted to named war-chest doctrine** — third data point after Jacobson + Erdemir); (ii) historical-tradition safe harbor; (iii) equity protects de facto officer pending litigation; (iv) notice adequacy three-part rule.

- **Watch thread:** whether the Sixth Circuit or Fifth Circuit tries to extend *Cook*'s historical-tradition carveout to other agencies (FDIC, OCC, SEC via *Free Enterprise Fund* residuum). Also watch: what happens on remand as the D.C. District Court applies the "substantial threshold" cause standard to Cook's alleged pre-office conduct.

sections.heading: Main Opinion Summary
sections.content_markdown: Chief Justice Roberts, writing for a five-Justice majority (joined by Sotomayor, Kagan, Kavanaugh, and Jackson), denies the President's application for a stay of the D.C. District Court's preliminary injunction restoring Governor Lisa Cook to her position on the Federal Reserve Board. In August 2025, President Trump purported to fire Cook — the first Federal Reserve Governor fired in the central bank's 111-year history — for alleged mortgage fraud committed shortly before she assumed office. The District Court enjoined the removal on due process and "for cause" statutory grounds; the D.C. Circuit refused a stay. **The Court holds:** (1) The Government has not shown likelihood of success on the merits. Federal Reserve Act § 242's "for cause" language is judicially reviewable (rejecting the Government's textual-commitment argument); "cause" is a common-law term of art requiring a substantial threshold reflecting the Federal Reserve's unique historical role; the President's determination is not immune from review. (2) On the narrowest available ground, the President failed to afford Cook the notice-and-opportunity-to-respond that fixed-term + for-cause statutes implicitly require under the *Reagan/Shurtleff* rule. Trump's social-media post did not constitute constitutionally adequate notice. (3) Federal courts have equitable authority to grant interim reinstatement pending merits litigation — *In re Sawyer* and *White v. Berry* addressed final equitable relief where legal remedies were adequate, not interim protection of *de facto* officers. (4) The Federal Reserve's statutory removal restriction is constitutional under Article II because the Federal Reserve follows in the "distinct historical tradition" of the First and Second Banks of the United States — an independence "sanctioned by history" that the Framers themselves guaranteed against Presidential control. Concurring opinions by Kavanaugh (emphasizing the risk of leaving the Federal Reserve's status open post-*Slaughter*) and Jackson (relying primarily on equitable factors). Dissents by Thomas (Article II forecloses any removal restriction on principal officers; statute is unconstitutional; equity cannot interfere with removal), Alito joined by Gorsuch (majority reached too many issues on an underdeveloped record), and Barrett (concurring in some critiques of scope; also arguing the injunction is broader than the majority acknowledges). **Application for stay DENIED.** Decided companion to [[trump_v_slaughter|Trump v. Slaughter]] the same day.

cases.title: Trump v. Slaughter, No. 25-332 (U.S. June 29, 2026)
cases.court: Supreme Court of the United States

sections.heading: 1. The Issue
sections.content_markdown: Whether the Federal Trade Commission Act's for-cause removal restriction on FTC Commissioners (15 U.S.C. § 41 — Commissioners removable only "for inefficiency, neglect of duty, or malfeasance in office") is consistent with the separation of powers under Article II of the Constitution. Sub-question, dispositive of the case: **whether *Humphrey's Executor v. United States*, 295 U.S. 602 (1935)**, which for 90 years has permitted for-cause protection of FTC-style multimember commissions on the theory that they exercise "quasi-legislative" and "quasi-judicial" (not "purely executive") powers, **should be overruled**.

sections.heading: 2. Rule Mapping
sections.content_markdown:
**Article II Vesting Clause + Take Care Clause:** "The executive Power shall be vested in a President of the United States of America." Art. II, § 1, cl. 1. President "shall take Care that the Laws be faithfully executed." Art. II, § 3. Together they establish, on the majority's reading, a **hierarchy** — Chief Magistrate with subordinates who "derive their offices from his appointment" and remain "subject to his superintendence." *The Federalist* No. 72, at 436 (Hamilton).

**The Decision of 1789:** During the First Congress's debate over structuring executive departments, Madison and allies successfully urged that "the power of removal" is part of "the Executive power" vested in the President, which "the Legislature has no right to diminish or modify." 1 Annals of Cong. 463. Bill establishing Department of Foreign Affairs was amended to assume (not confer) Presidential removal power. "Regular course of practice" that "liquidate[d] & settle[d]" the President's power of removal. 8 Writings of James Madison 450 (Hunt ed.). Confirmed by *Ex parte Hennen*, 13 Pet. 230, 259 (1839), and *Parsons v. United States*, 167 U.S. 324, 330 (1897).

***Myers v. United States*, 272 U.S. 52 (1926):** Chief Justice Taft's landmark opinion holding that the President has constitutional power to remove executive officers without Senate consent, notwithstanding contrary statute. "The vesting of the executive power in the President was essentially a grant of the power to execute the laws," including power to "remov[e] those for whom he can not continue to be responsible." *Id.* at 117. **Rule: President's removal power flows from Article II and cannot be modified by Congress for principal executive officers.**

***Humphrey's Executor v. United States*, 295 U.S. 602 (1935) — OVERRULED TODAY:** Distinguished *Myers* by holding that FTC Commissioners exercise "no part of the executive power," but rather "quasi-legislative" and "quasi-judicial" functions. Because these were not "purely executive" powers, Congress could impose for-cause removal restrictions. *Id.* at 624, 628. **The rule the Court today discards** as "a result in search of a rationale."

**Modern removal doctrine (developed 2010–2020):**
- *Free Enterprise Fund v. PCAOB*, 561 U.S. 477 (2010): Refused to extend *Humphrey's* to two-layered for-cause protection; struck down as "novel structure."
- *Seila Law LLC v. CFPB*, 591 U.S. 197 (2020): Refused to extend *Humphrey's* to single-director agency; described *Humphrey's* as limited to "an expert agency led by a group of principal officers removable by the President only for good cause."
- *Collins v. Yellen*, 594 U.S. 220 (2021): Continued cabining of *Humphrey's*.

**Stare decisis framework (Knick factors):** Quality of decision's reasoning; consistency with other cases; workability of rule; reliance interests. *Knick v. Township of Scott*, 588 U.S. 180, 203 (2019).

**Historical-tradition carveout (post-*Slaughter*):** Federal Reserve preserved as constitutional exception "to the extent that it follows in the distinct historical tradition of the First and Second Banks of the United States." *Seila Law*, 591 U.S. at 222, n. 8, made load-bearing today. Non-Article III courts (Tax Court, Court of Federal Claims) reserved for another day.

sections.heading: 3. Synthesis: The "Why" and "So What"
sections.content_markdown:
**The Why.** *Slaughter* is the case that formally overrules *Humphrey's Executor* — a 91-year-old precedent that structured the modern administrative state — and thereby establishes as constitutional doctrine what the Court has been telegraphing for a decade: **principal officers exercising executive power must be removable by the President at will**. The majority's path travels three doctrinal moves, each of which is worth tracing separately because they operate at different altitudes:

- **Text + history + structure (Part II-A).** The majority reads Article II's vesting clause, in light of Convention debates and the Decision of 1789, to require a unitary executive with unbroken removal authority. Key structural principle: "unity" of the executive would be "destroyed" if it were subject to "the control and co-operation of others" (Federalist No. 70). Executive officers are "assistants or deputies" who "derive their offices from [the President's] appointment" and are subject to his "superintendence." *Federalist No. 72*. Because officers exercise the President's power (not their own), they must be removable by him. The Decision of 1789 is treated as a "liquidation" (Madison's word) of the Constitution's meaning — a founding-era consensus that the majority reads as binding. *Cf.* *Chiafalo v. Washington*, 591 U.S. 578 (2020).

- **Precedent (Part II-B): *Myers* right, *Humphrey's* wrong.** The majority rehabilitates *Myers* (previously narrowed as concerning only Senate-consent restrictions) as controlling authority for a broad rule of at-will removal. *Humphrey's* is characterized as a "result in search of a rationale" whose "quasi-legislative/quasi-judicial" framework "has not withstood the test of time." Justice Scalia's dissent in *Morrison v. Olson*, 487 U.S. 654, 725 (1988) — the "dustbin of repudiated constitutional principles" line — is invoked as vindicated. *Humphrey's* is now "far out of step with our cases, which have all but limited it to its facts."

- **Stare decisis (Part II-B(2)(c)): every *Knick* factor cuts against retention.** Quality of reasoning: *Humphrey's* transformed executive powers into "quasi" functions "only by blind feats of definition" (quoting Peter Strauss). Consistency: *Humphrey's* has been retreated from at every turn. Workability: no one knows how to apply *Humphrey's* — lower courts have splintered on whether it protects all multimember agencies, only bipartisan ones, only those matching the 1935 FTC exactly, etc. Reliance: the majority acknowledges Congress relied on *Humphrey's* to create dozens of independent agencies but characterizes this as "the reliance interests of the American people . . . in the preservation of our constitutionally promised liberties" — reframing reliance to run in favor of overruling.

**The historical-tradition carveout is deliberately narrow.** The majority takes care in Part III-B to preserve the Federal Reserve, activated in the companion case *Trump v. Cook*, as a constitutional exception based on its lineage from the First and Second Banks of the United States. Non-Article III courts (Tax Court, Court of Federal Claims) are also reserved. Everything else — every other multimember independent commission with for-cause protection (SEC, NLRB, MSPB, NRC, FERC, CPSC, FCC, and dozens more) — is now presumptively subject to Presidential at-will removal.

**Gorsuch's concurrence carries the deeper structural warning.** Congress delegated so much legislative and judicial power to "independent" agencies in reliance on *Humphrey's*'s independence guarantee. Now that the independence is gone, the President inherits vast legislative and judicial functions along with executive control. Gorsuch flags this as ratchet-effect: authorities Congress once delegated by simple majority "may now require a veto-proof supermajority to retrieve." His prescription: revive nondelegation, sharpen the major questions doctrine, apply *Jarkesy*/Seventh Amendment discipline to in-house adjudications. "It is time to return, all the way, to the Constitution."

**Sotomayor's dissent (joined by Kagan and Jackson) is the historical + stare decisis counterpunch.** The dissent's structural argument: (a) text of Article II says nothing about removal power; (b) Framers explicitly did not give the President a removal power the English Crown lacked; (c) the Decision of 1789 addressed *Senate consent* to removals (rejected), not *fixed-term protections* (unresolved); (d) 140 years of continuous practice — beginning with the ICC (1887), Board of General Appraisers (1890), Federal Reserve Board (1913), FTC (1914), and continuing to dozens of modern agencies — shows the political branches have jointly settled the constitutional question in favor of Congressional power to impose modest removal limits. On stare decisis: *Humphrey's* generated 91 years of massive reliance; the overruling is "a profound bait and switch on a coequal branch."

**The So What.** Four propositions land as usable precedent:

1. ***Humphrey's Executor* is overruled.** The Court is explicit: "If anything more is left of *Humphrey's*, we overrule it." Multimember independent commissions with for-cause protection are presumptively unconstitutional. Any current commissioner subject to for-cause protection can be removed at will by the President; any pending litigation seeking to enforce such protections will lose on the merits.

2. **The unitary executive theory is now doctrine, not aspiration.** All powers within the "heartland of executive power" (rulemaking against private parties, enforcement, civil suits, in-house adjudications regulating private conduct) must be exercised by officers removable by the President at will. This is a formalist Article II holding — "no ifs, ands, or quasis about it" (Sotomayor dissent quoting the majority).

3. **The historical-tradition carveout is real but narrow.** Federal Reserve preserved (via *Cook*); non-Article III courts reserved; congressionally-chartered entities without executive/sovereign power (Boy Scouts, Georgetown University) unaffected. Any other agency claiming carveout must show founding-era pedigree analogous to central banking — a very high bar the majority does not explicitly define. Expect litigation testing the edges (FDIC? OCC? SEC? PCAOB post-*Free Enterprise Fund*?).

4. **Reliance-interests analysis for stare decisis is now malleable.** The majority reframes Congress's reliance on *Humphrey's* as *illegitimate* reliance ("no branch may rely on adverse possession to claim power the Constitution vests elsewhere") and elevates a competing category — "reliance interests of the American people . . . in the preservation of our constitutionally promised liberties" — as controlling. This move is portable: whenever a majority believes a longstanding precedent is constitutionally wrong, the reliance analysis can be recast in favor of overruling.

Downstream, cascading: expect executive orders removing commissioners across dozens of agencies; legal challenges dismissed on the merits under *Slaughter*; Congressional consideration of statutory workarounds (though Gorsuch's ratchet-effect note suggests these are unlikely to survive Presidential veto); litigation testing whether inferior officers and civil-service employees fall within *Slaughter*'s logic (majority does not resolve; Sotomayor dissent flags as open); and litigation over which agencies (if any) can claim the Federal Reserve's historical-tradition carveout.

sections.heading: 4. Logical Flaws & Observations
sections.content_markdown:
- **The move that is doing the most work: reframing reliance.** *Slaughter*'s stare decisis analysis has to overcome 91 years of legislative and Executive Branch reliance on *Humphrey's*. The majority does this by (a) characterizing Congress's reliance as illegitimate ("adverse possession") and (b) inventing a competing reliance category ("the American people . . . in the preservation of our constitutionally promised liberties"). Both moves are contestable. Congress relied on *Humphrey's* because *this Court* told Congress to. And the "American people's" reliance is asserted, not demonstrated — no polling, no historical evidence, no showing that the public understood their liberties to require unitary Presidential control of the FTC. **This is the load-bearing rhetorical move**, and it's worth naming as such because the same move will be available in future stare-decisis overrulings.

- **Gorsuch's concurrence is the most doctrinally consequential opinion in the case.** The majority overrules *Humphrey's*. Gorsuch says (correctly) that this is only the first step — the harder problem is that Congress delegated so much legislative and judicial power to "independent" agencies. Now the President inherits all of it. Gorsuch's answer is to revive nondelegation, major questions, and Article III/Seventh Amendment doctrine to strip agencies of the *powers* Congress gave them. **If the concurrence's program becomes majority in future terms, the administrative state as we know it does not survive.** This is a signal worth naming — Gorsuch is explicit that "it is time to return, all the way, to the Constitution."

- **The "quasi" terminology critique cuts both ways.** The majority mocks *Humphrey's* "retreat to the qualifying 'quasi'" (Justice Jackson's line from *FTC v. Ruberoid Co.*, 343 U.S. 470, 487–488 (1952)). But administrative law has long treated agencies as exercising functionally distinct powers even where formally executive — that's the whole edifice of *Chevron*, *Skidmore*, APA § 706 review, agency adjudication under *Wong Yang Sung* and *Butz v. Economou*, etc. Overruling *Humphrey's* does not resolve the underlying doctrinal question of how to categorize hybrid agency functions; it just declares by fiat that all such functions are "executive." That declaration will produce line-drawing problems the majority doesn't address.

- **The historical-tradition carveout is untheorized.** The majority preserves the Federal Reserve as an exception "sanctioned by history." Sotomayor's dissent presses the obvious question: what other agencies qualify? What is the test? The majority declines to say. The concurrence and dissents in *Cook* highlight that this is a new (or newly-explicit) doctrinal category — "general rule + historical-tradition carveout" — that will require future development. Barrett's dissent in *Cook* names it precisely: "history [used] to support both a categorical rule and a carveout" is an unstable structure.

- **Party presentation at the certiorari-before-judgment stage.** *Slaughter* is a certiorari-before-judgment case, granted after a District Court summary judgment and a D.C. Circuit stay denial. That's a very thin record for overruling a 91-year-old precedent. The majority relies heavily on scholarly work, prior dissents, and its own recent removal cases. Alito's dissent in *Cook* names the concern: emergency-docket and expedited-cert cases risk deciding too much on too little.

- **The Decision of 1789 as constitutional-interpretation tool.** The majority reads the Decision of 1789 as a Madisonian "liquidation" of the Constitution's meaning — founding-era practice becomes controlling constitutional interpretation. Sotomayor's dissent contests both the historical record (the Decision addressed Senate consent, not tenure protection) and the interpretive theory (post-ratification practice can inform but not fix constitutional meaning). This methodological dispute — how much weight founding-era practice deserves — recurs across constitutional law. *Slaughter* is a strong pro-liquidation opinion; watch for it to shape future methodological debates.

sections.heading: 5. Follow-ups for Mr. C
sections.content_markdown:
- **Doctrinal-scaffold angle (for JR's returning-paralegal register):** The core stare-decisis move in *Slaughter* is a **four-factor overruling framework** (Knick): quality, consistency, workability, reliance. Notable that the majority runs all four and finds each cuts against *Humphrey's* — this is the *maximalist* overruling posture. Compare to overrulings that rely on fewer factors (*Dobbs* emphasized quality + workability; *Loper Bright* emphasized quality + reliance-recast). **The lesson worth naming:** the Knick framework is malleable in application; different overrulings emphasize different factors, and reliance-reframing has become a portable technique.

- **Weight-of-words angle:** "Quasi" is doing enormous rhetorical work in this case — mocked by the majority (Jackson's "counterpane to conceal a disordered bed" line), defended by the dissent as a "well-established term-of-art" with pedigree from Madison through Taft. The word carries the entire administrative-state settlement: whether agency functions can be *partially* legislative or judicial, or must be *categorically* executive. **When the same word is used mockingly by one opinion and treated as load-bearing by another, that word is the case.** Same pattern as "reasonable" in tort law (mocked as vacuous, defended as necessary) and "just and equitable" in equity (same).

- **Structural analogy (rigid statutes, flexible analysis — inverted):** *Slaughter* is the *inverse* of your rigid-statute-flexible-analysis observation. Here the *Constitution* is treated as rigid (Article II vesting = unitary executive removal) and the *analysis* of what falls within its scope is treated as expansive (all "executive" power). Compare to *Cook*, decided the same day, which does the opposite — flexible constitutional analysis (historical-tradition carveout) reading a rigid-looking statute ("for cause" as substantial-threshold requirement). **The two cases together illustrate that "rigid vs. flexible" is not a property of the text; it's a property of the interpretive move.**

- **The historical-tradition safe harbor as a doctrine to watch.** *Slaughter* announces a general rule; *Cook* activates the Federal Reserve carveout. This is a **general-rule + historical-tradition-carveout** doctrinal shape, and it is likely to recur wherever the Court's structural theory conflicts with longstanding practice. Analogous shape: *Rahimi*'s "consistent with the principles that underpin the historical tradition" test in Second Amendment law. Same doctrinal move: rigid modern rule + flexible historical safe harbor. **Worth naming as a category** because it applies wherever originalism meets long-practice-based reliance.

- **Cross-stitch with prior corpus.** *Slaughter* is the top of the removal-law tree. All future work on Article II, independent agencies, civil-service protection, MSPB status, adjudicative-body independence, and nondelegation-adjacent doctrine traces back here. Pair with [[trump_v_cook|*Trump v. Cook*]] as the immediate companion. **Recommend an SR Sharpens synthesis pass** treating *Slaughter* + *Cook* as a paired doctrinal unit — the two cases are inseparable and should be studied together, not in isolation.

- **Field Clerk paired 2026-07-11:** [[25-332|Field Clerk Draft — 25-332 (Trump v. Slaughter)]] at `Terminal/Central Hub/research/data/legal/field-clerks/25-332.md`. Isolates five carry-forward doctrines: (i) **reliance-reframing move** as portable stare-decisis technique (high-priority promotion candidate); (ii) Knick four-factor malleability; (iii) general-rule + historical-tradition-carveout (paired to Cook FC); (iv) Decision of 1789 as constitutional liquidation; (v) "quasi" as name-light rejection without functional replacement.

- **Watch thread — the concurrence.** Gorsuch's concurrence explicitly signals the next moves: nondelegation revival, major questions expansion, *Jarkesy*/Seventh Amendment application to more agencies. If a majority forms around any of these programs in future terms, the administrative state contracts sharply. Track this concurrence across future cases — Gorsuch will be the vote to watch, and the concurrence is a roadmap.

- **Watch thread — inferior officers and civil service.** The Government at argument conceded the "logic" of its position "extends to inferior officers" and perhaps civil servants. The majority does not decide this. Sotomayor's dissent flags it as an open door. Expect challenges to civil-service protections (5 U.S.C. §§ 7511–7513) and to MSPB independence within a term or two.

sections.heading: Main Opinion Summary
sections.content_markdown: Chief Justice Roberts, writing for a six-Justice majority (Alito, Gorsuch, Kavanaugh, Barrett joining in full; Thomas joining all but Part III-B), reverses the D.C. District Court's summary judgment restoring FTC Commissioner Rebecca Slaughter to her position and holds that the FTC Commissioners' for-cause removal protection (15 U.S.C. § 41) is unconstitutional under Article II of the Constitution. President Trump had fired Slaughter and fellow Democratic Commissioner Alvaro Bedoya without asserting statutory cause, citing only Article II authority; the District Court declared the removals ultra vires under *Humphrey's Executor v. United States*, 295 U.S. 602 (1935), and the D.C. Circuit denied a stay. **The Court holds:** (1) Article II's Vesting Clause and Take Care Clause vest all executive power in the President, requiring subordinate officers who exercise executive power to be removable by him at will. Text, Founding-era history, the Decision of 1789, and *Myers v. United States*, 272 U.S. 52 (1926), all support this rule. (2) *Humphrey's Executor* is overruled. Its "quasi-legislative/quasi-judicial" framework is "a result in search of a rationale" that fails every stare decisis factor (quality, consistency, workability, reliance). Justice Scalia's characterization of *Humphrey's* as belonging in the "dustbin of repudiated constitutional principles" (*Morrison v. Olson* dissent) is vindicated. (3) The FTC exercises quintessentially executive power — rulemaking, enforcement, in-house adjudication, civil suits — and its Commissioners must therefore be removable by the President at will. (4) A **historical-tradition carveout** preserves the Federal Reserve (via *Trump v. Cook*, decided the same day) as an exception "sanctioned by history" based on its lineage from the First and Second Banks of the United States. Non-Article III courts (Tax Court, Court of Federal Claims) are reserved for another day. Justice Gorsuch's concurrence emphasizes that overruling *Humphrey's* is only the first step — Congress delegated vast legislative and judicial powers to now-formerly-independent agencies, and the Court should revive nondelegation, major questions, and Article III/Seventh Amendment doctrine to restore those powers to Congress and the courts. Justice Sotomayor's dissent (joined by Kagan and Jackson) argues the majority ignores 91 years of precedent, 140 years of continuous political practice, and massive congressional reliance interests, elevating a rigid theory of unitary executive control over the Constitution's actual text and the settled understanding of coequal branches. **Judgment REVERSED and REMANDED.** Decided companion to [[trump_v_cook|Trump v. Cook]] the same day.
